An unseasonably cold air mass for late October coupled with weak waves of low pressure moving east across the Tennessee Valley and the Carolinas brought the region's first wintry mix of the season. Precipitation fell as a mixture of snow, sleet and freezing rain over the higher terrain of Lehigh, Northampton, Carbon and Monroe Counties during the late afternoon and the night of the 29th. By the morning of the 30th, precipitation fell as rain, except for the higher terrain in the Poconos where light snow persisted. The highest accumulations occurred across the higher terrain in southern Carbon and Monroe Counties and mainly on grassy areas. Since the ground was still warm, few traffic related problems occurred. Specific accumulations included 3 inches in Saylorsburg (Monroe County), 2 inches in Tamaqua (Schuylkill County), 1.2 inches in Bath (Northampton County), 1 inch at the F.E. Walter Dam in Carbon County and 0.5 inches in Forks Township (Northampton County).

In addition to the severe weather, the thunderstorms dropped heavy rain as they moved through Philadelphia. This caused rapid flooding along the Frankford Creek. The Frankford Creek at Castor Avenue was above its 7 foot flood stage from 732 p.m. EDT through 813 p.m. EDT. It crested at 9.62 feet at 745 p.m. EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The excessive heat that began on July 28th continued through August 5th. The combination of high temperatures in the 90s and high dew points produced heat indices of over 100 each day through the 5th.  A cold frontal passage late in the day on the 5th ended the excessive heat. The hottest day of this spell for most places in August was the second with high temperatures reaching or exceeding 100F in many places.The excessive heat caused nine more heat related deaths in and around Philadelphia. Three deaths each occurred in Chester, Montgomery and Philadelphia counties. Seven of the deaths were under similar circumstances: persons found in their apartments with no air conditioning or no air conditioning running and room temperatures exceeding 90 degrees. A 91-year-old woman in East Norriton (Montgomery County) and and a 74-year-old woman in Philadelphia were found dead outside their homes. The excessive heat also produced record demand for electricity. Pennsylvania Power and Light set a new daily usage record (6,754 megawatts) on the 2nd and PECO Energy set a new weekend usage record on the 3rd and 4th. The heat caused electrical transformers to pop. PECO Energy reported about 26,000 customers lost power. The heat caused systemwide delays on the SEPTA commuter rail lines because of sagging lines. In Chester County, the eastbound lanes of U.S. Route 30 were closed for about three hours on the first because the heat caused the road to heave.   Highest temperatures included 102 degrees at the Franklin Institute within Philadelphia, Willow Grove (Montgomery County), Perkasie (Bucks County), West Chester (Chester County) and Bath (Northampton County), 101 degrees in Reading (Berks County), Lehighton (Carbon County) and Valley Forge (Chester County), 100 degrees in Hamburg (Berks County) and Neshaminy Falls (Bucks County), 99 degrees at the Philadelphia and Lehigh Valley International Airports and Sellersville (Bucks County), 98 degrees in East Stroudsburg (Monroe County) and 91 degrees in Tobyhanna (Monroe County).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the mid morning hours of Tuesday October 29th 2002, a mixture of wet snow, sleet and rain developed across much of Central Pennsylvania. This mixture occurred at moderate intensity through much of Tuesday afternoon and evening, and continued intermittently through the evening hours of Wednesday, October 30th 2002. Total snowfall accumulations of 1 to 4 inches occurred across Centre County, with ice accretion from freezing rain of up to one inch across higher elevations (generally above 2000 feet above mean sea level) of Cambria, Somerset, Fulton, Franklin, and Bedford counties. With trees still containing nearly full foliage, the added weight of ice and heavy, wet snow downed numerous trees and power lines across the area. This created widespread power outages, particularly in Centre County, where Allegheny Power reported approximately 16,800 customers without power.  The State College area was particularly hard hit with downed trees and power loss. Additional power outages occurred through the higher elevations of Cambria, Somerset, Bedford, Fulton and Franklin counties.  Many customers remained without power through Thursday, October 31st 2002. In addition, hundreds of downed trees were reported in higher elevations of Fulton, Franklin, and Somerset Counties.  One ski resort near Bakersville in Somerset County reported extensive tree damage, amounting to at least one hundred thousand dollars.  Numerous roads across these higher elevations were closed due to downed trees, and the added danger of falling ice.
A tree fell on a pickup truck on Old Route 115 in the town of Lehman.  The tree crushed the cab of the truck injuring the two occupants.

A 16-year-old boy scout was struck and killed by lightning while attending the Resica Falls Boy Scout Camp off Pennsylvania State Route 402 in Middle Smithfield Township. Lightning apparently struck a tent where he and eleven other scouts were eating. Six other scouts suffered minor injuries.
Dime to nickel size hail fell in Forks Township.

A severe thunderstorm knocked down numerous trees and wires throughout Lehigh County. The greatest damage occurred south and west of Allentown. Several large trees were knocked down in Lower Macungie Township. Western Salisbury Township was also hard hit with many trees and wires down. About a half dozen roads were closed including the 24th Street Bridge between the township and Allentown. Pennsylvania Power and Light reported about 9,700 of its customers lost power because of the storm with the greatest concentration in Lehigh County in southern Allentown and Emmaus. Only a third of their customers had power restored by 1030 p.m. EDT that evening and power was completely restored to the last 1,000 on the 4th. Five people in Allentown were rescued from a stuck elevator after the power was lost.

October 2002 brought to Eastern Pennsylvania the first widespread wetter than normal month since March. Every county was wetter than normal and in most cases received over twice the normal monthly precipitation for October.  On October 10th and 11th, more rain fell across the region than is normal for the entire month. Groundwater monitoring wells in Montgomery and Bucks Counties jumped a foot after the heavy rain. October is usually the driest month of the year. Most of Eastern Pennsylvania remained under drought emergency status with mandatory watering restrictions in place. Residents were required to conserve water by 15 percent and could face penalties for violating regulations. Only Carbon and Monroe Counties were in drought watch status. The Delaware River Basin Commission, which oversees water supply throughout the Delaware River Basin declared a "drought" on December 3, 2001 when the supply of water in the New York City reservoir system dropped below a designated level for early December. The commission declared a drought emergency on December 18, 2001. Both triggered additional reductions in the amount of water released from the reservoirs into the Delaware River and the amount diverted out of the Delaware River Basin to New York City and New Jersey. Diversions to New York City dropped from 800 million gallons a day to 520 million gallons a day.  The emergency declaration gave the commission the power to restrict the amount of water suppliers could draw from the basin. It gave the commission the authority to take water from municipal reservoirs to maintain river levels. It could also call for water releases from federal, state and privately owned reservoirs. Water from Lake Nockamixon, the F.E. Walter Reservoir and Lake Wallenpaupack could be used to maintain river levels. Releases from the F.E. Walter Dam were reduced to one-third of normal to save water. They were last used for this purpose in the 1960s. The releases help protect the riverbank and aquatic life and also prevents salt water from flowing up the Delaware River. Salt water intrusion causes corrosion problems for industries and increases water treatment costs for municipalities that depend on the river for their water supply. Maintaining river levels are also important for groundwater reserves that are recharged by the river itself.  Due to the heavy rain during October, the salt front went from mile marker 88 on October 1st to mile marker 68 on October 31st, 13 miles farther downstream than normal for October. The emergency declaration also gives the commission the power to request that utilities and other companies that use more than one million gallons of water a day to prepare contingency plans to use less water and submit the plans to the commission. The heavy precipitation in October came too late to help farmers throughout the state. It was estimated that the drought will cost farmers about 300 million dollars in Pennsylvania. The projected corn harvest should be 30 percent below levels of last year and soybeans were expected to be down about 25 percent. Dairy farmers were pinched on both sides. Milk prices were depressed and feed prices were rising because of the drought.  On a county weighted average October monthly precipitation totals ranged from 5.7 inches in Bucks County to 8.3 inches in Carbon County. More precipitation fell from the south to the north. Normal monthly precipitation is around 3.2 inches. The New York City Reservoirs which feed the Delaware River increased from 50.4 percent of capacity on October 1st to 57.6 percent of capacity on November 1st. Late October was the first time since early 2001 that reservoir capacity was higher than normal. On October 31st, the salt front on the Delaware River was about thirteen miles farther downstream (mile marker 68) than normal. For the month stream flow on the Delaware, Schuylkill and Lehigh Rivers was about 200 percent of  normal. October monthly precipitation was 7.20 inches at the Reading Regional Airport, 6.85 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport (4th wettest on record) and 5.90 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport (5th wettest on record).

A slow moving warm front moved slowly north along the Atlantic Seaboard on the 10th through the 12th. Warm tropical air forced aloft over this boundary resulted in heavy rain across Eastern Pennsylvania on the 10th and 11th. This caused poor drainage flooding.  The rain was beneficial in starting the replenishing process in the drought stricken area. Monitoring wells in Bucks and Montgomery Counties rebounded one foot after this event. Most places received their normal October precipitation complement from this one precipitation event. A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees and wires in Birmingham Township.

A severe thunderstorm knocked over several trees and power lines across southeastern Berks County. About 1,700 homes and businesses lost power in Boyertown. A downed tree blocked Pennsylvania State Route 662 in Amity Township. A woman drove into the downed tree, but was not seriously injured.
A severe thunderstorm knocked down several trees and power lines in central Berks County. About 1,500 homes and businesses in Maiden Creek Township lost power.
A severe thunderstorm knocked over a couple of trees in far northwestern Montgomery County. A couple of downed large trees blocked roadways in Limerick Township.
A severe thunderstorm knocked down trees in Elverson.

The longest run of days with high temperatures of 90 or greater this summer occurred across Eastern Pennsylvania from the 11th through the 20th. This was the longest heat wave for the state since the twelve day heat wave during the summer of 1999. It also solidified August and the summer of 2002 as one of the ten hottest summers on record. Along with the heat came the humidity. The hottest weather occurred for most places on the 13th and 14th. A couple of weak cold fronts dissipated as they tried to cross the state during this heat wave. The only effect was to keep temperatures and humidity levels from getting any higher from the 15th through the 20th. A stronger cold front moved through the state on the 20th and ended the run of hot weather.  PECO Energy set a new usage record. There were eight additional heat related deaths in the city of Philadelphia during this hot spell. This brought the city's heat related death total to 34. A 20-month-old toddler died after her grandfather accidently left her in his car for eight hours. The thirty-four heat related deaths was lower than similar hot summers during the early and mid 1990s. Thousands of acres of flat roofed attached housing can be turned into ovens during hot summers in urbanized areas. On days when excessive heat warnings are in effect, the Philadelphia Health Department issues heat alerts and coordinates an active heat outreach program to the elderly and homeless. Six thousand block captains are notified and asked to check on elderly neighbors. A heat hot line is activated to answer questions. The city contracts with Project Home, a private outreach program that provides water and support to the city's homeless. Group living homes are also required to keep room temperatures below 81 degrees. Most of the heat relate deaths, even those that perished during this heat wave, occur to  the poor and isolated elderly. They tend to live in high crime and abandoned neighborhoods. Fear of  crime keeps their windows and doors shut. Highest temperatures included 100 degrees in Reading (Berks County), 99 degrees in Stroudsburg (Monroe County) and the Philadelphia and Lehigh Valley International Airports, 98 degrees in Doylestown (Bucks County) and 97 degrees in Pottstown (Montgomery County).

A forest fire consumed about 100 acres of forest and underbrush near Blue Mountain in Lehigh Township. Lightning apparently initiated the fire just south of the Appalachian Trail. It spread quickly because of the dead underbrush caused by the drought. About 500 firefighters battled the blaze. Blue Mountain Drive was closed on the 3rd and part of the 4th until the fire was contained.

A severe thunderstorm knocked down trees and wires across southern parts of Northampton County hitting locations from Forks Township south through Williams Township and Bethlehem City the hardest. Pennsylvania State Route 611 in Forks Township was closed for about 30 minutes while road crews cleared the downed trees. A portable bathroom was displaced onto a street. In Easton, a utility pole was snapped. In Williams Township, fallen trees closed two roads and damaged one vehicle. Pennsylvania Power and Light reported about 9,700 of its customers lost power because of the storm with the greatest concentration in Northampton County in Bethlehem. Only a third of their customers had power restored by 1030 p.m. EDT that evening and power was completely restored to the last 1,000 on the 4th.  Met Ed reported about 3,400 of their customers also lost power in Easton.
A severe thunderstorm knocked down trees in Sinking Spring.
A severe thunderstorm knocked over some trees in East Bradford Township.

A severe thunderstorm that straddled the Montgomery/Bucks County line knocked down several trees and numerous power lines in each county. A tremendous number of cloud-to-ground lightning strikes accompanied this storm: about 7,500 cloud-to-ground lightning strikes were reported in the Greater Philadelphia Metropolitan Area and accounted for the bulk of the tree damage in both counties. PECO Energy reported about 100,000 of its customers lost power in both counties. All power was restored by the 4th.

PECO Energy reported about 100,000 of its customers lost power in both counties. All power was restored by the 4th.

A pair of lightning strikes caused damage in Newtown and Lower Makefield Townships. In Lower Makefield Township, a lightning strike damaged half of a house's roof. In Newtown, a lightning strike resulted in a fire later that evening in a barn located in the Rosewood Winery property. It took firefighters one hour to get the fire under control.

A lightning strike during the evening of the 2nd was the attributed cause of an empty warehouse fire that developed during the early morning of the 3rd. It took firefighters one hour to get the fire under control.

The excessive heat that began on July 28th continued through August 5th. The combination of high temperatures in the 90s and high dew points produced heat indices of over 100 each day through the 5th.  A cold frontal passage late in the day on the 5th ended the excessive heat. The hottest day of this spell for most places in August was the second with high temperatures reaching or exceeding 100F in many places.The excessive heat caused nine more heat related deaths in and around Philadelphia. Three deaths each occurred in Chester, Montgomery and Philadelphia counties. Seven of the deaths were under similar circumstances: persons found in their apartments with no air conditioning or no air conditioning running and room temperatures exceeding 90 degrees. A 91-year-old woman in East Norriton (Montgomery County) and and a 74-year-old woman in Philadelphia were found dead outside their homes. The excessive heat also produced record demand for electricity. Pennsylvania Power and Light set a new daily usage record (6,754 megawatts) on the 2nd and PECO Energy set a new weekend usage record on the 3rd and 4th. The heat caused electrical transformers to pop. PECO Energy reported about 26,000 customers lost power. The heat caused systemwide delays on the SEPTA commuter rail lines because of sagging lines. In Chester County, the eastbound lanes of U.S. Route 30 were closed for about three hours on the first because the heat caused the road to heave.   Highest temperatures included 102 degrees at the Franklin Institute within Philadelphia, Willow Grove (Montgomery County), Perkasie (Bucks County), West Chester (Chester County) and Bath (Northampton County), 101 degrees in Reading (Berks County), Lehighton (Carbon County) and Valley Forge (Chester County), 100 degrees in Hamburg (Berks County) and Neshaminy Falls (Bucks County), 99 degrees at the Philadelphia and Lehigh Valley International Airports and Sellersville (Bucks County), 98 degrees in East Stroudsburg (Monroe County) and 91 degrees in Tobyhanna (Monroe County).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Greensburg accumulated 6 inches of snow from the afternoon of the 26th to the morning of the 27th.

Thunderstorms dumped up to 3.25 inches of rain in less than two hours causing widespread flooding across Erie County.  Water up to three feet in depth was reported on some roads with several additional roads washed out.
